seo.title: CSS Display and Positioning - block, inline, position: relative/absolute/fixed/sticky, z-index | Modern Age Coders
seo.description: Master CSS display and positioning. Learn block, inline, inline-block, none, visibility: hidden, position: static, relative, absolute, fixed, sticky, top/right/bottom/left, z-index stacking, and overflow. 58+ practice questions.
seo.keywords: css display, css position, position absolute, position fixed, position sticky, z-index css, css overflow, inline-block css, visibility hidden, sticky navbar css

content.what:
What Are Display and Positioning?

Display and positioning control two fundamental questions about every element:

  1. display: How does this element flow in the page? Like a block (new line, full width), inline (in the text flow), or not at all (removed)?
  2. position: Where exactly is this element placed? In normal flow, offset from where it would be, or at a specific point on the screen?

Together they let you create sticky navbars, modal overlays, floating buttons, dropdowns, tooltips, fixed sidebars, and countless other UI patterns. These properties power almost every layout trick before flexbox/grid were invented, and they are still essential today for specific components.

/* display controls flow */
.btn { display: inline-block; }  /* flows like text but has width/height */
.hidden { display: none; }        /* gone */

/* position controls placement */
.modal { position: fixed; top: 50%; left: 50%; }  /* pinned to viewport */
.nav   { position: sticky; top: 0; }              /* sticks when scrolling */
content.why:
Why Do Display and Positioning Matter?

1. Every Layout Decision Uses Them

Do you want items in a row? Change their display. Do you want a button to float over the content? Change its position. Even with flexbox and grid for the main layout, positioning is essential for elements that sit on top of others: modals, tooltips, dropdowns, sticky headers, floating action buttons.

2. Position Is the Only Way to Overlap

In normal flow, elements never overlap. To make one element sit on top of another -- like a dropdown below a button, or a modal in the center of the screen -- you need position: absolute or position: fixed. Understanding this unlocks a huge category of UI.

3. Sticky Navbars Are Everywhere

Every modern website has a navbar that stays at the top of the screen when you scroll. The magic word is position: sticky. One property, and your navbar works perfectly.

4. display: none vs visibility: hidden Matters

Both hide elements. But one removes them from layout (display: none) and the other keeps the space (visibility: hidden). Choosing wrong creates awkward layout jumps when you toggle visibility.
content.explanation:
Detailed Explanation

1. display: block

Block elements start on a new line and take the full width available. They respect width, height, margin, and padding fully. Examples: <div>, <h1>, <p>, <section>, <header>.

h1 { display: block; }  /* default for headings */
/* Takes full width, starts on a new line */

2. display: inline

Inline elements flow with text, like words in a paragraph. They do NOT accept width or height. They only take as much space as their content. Examples: <span>, <a>, <strong>, <em>.

a { display: inline; }  /* default for links */
/* Flows in text. Ignores width/height. */

3. display: inline-block

The best of both worlds: flows in a row like inline, but accepts width, height, padding, and margin like block. Great for navigation items and buttons before flexbox existed.

.nav-item {
  display: inline-block;
  width: 100px;
  padding: 12px 20px;
  /* Flows in a row AND has a fixed size */
}

4. display: none

Completely removes the element from the layout. Other elements act as if it does not exist.

.hidden { display: none; }
/* Element is invisible AND does not take space */

5. visibility: hidden

Hides the element visually but KEEPS its space in the layout. Like an invisible placeholder.

.invisible { visibility: hidden; }
/* Invisible but still takes up its box */

Key difference:

  • display: none -- gone, space collapses, other elements move up
  • visibility: hidden -- invisible but the space is still there, layout does not shift

6. position: static (Default)

The default for all elements. They flow normally. top/right/bottom/left have no effect.

7. position: relative

The element still flows normally, BUT you can offset it with top/right/bottom/left. It leaves a 'ghost' where it used to be.

.nudged {
  position: relative;
  top: 10px;
  left: 20px;
  /* Moved 10px down and 20px right from its normal position */
}

Also: making a parent position: relative creates a positioning context for absolute children.

8. position: absolute

Removes the element from normal flow. It floats on top and is positioned relative to the nearest positioned ancestor (an ancestor with position: relative, absolute, fixed, or sticky). If none exists, it uses the viewport.

.parent { position: relative; }
.child  {
  position: absolute;
  top: 10px;
  right: 10px;
  /* Pinned to the top-right of the parent */
}

Absolutely positioned elements are removed from normal flow -- other elements act like they do not exist.

9. position: fixed

Pinned to the VIEWPORT (browser window). It stays in the same position on the screen even when you scroll. Perfect for floating action buttons, modals, and some navbars.

.fab {
  position: fixed;
  bottom: 30px;
  right: 30px;
  /* Bottom-right of the screen always, even when scrolling */
}

10. position: sticky

Hybrid: behaves like relative until you scroll past a threshold, then behaves like fixed. Perfect for sticky navbars and table headers.

nav {
  position: sticky;
  top: 0;
  /* Scrolls with the page until it hits the top, then sticks */
}

Sticky requires a defined threshold (top, left, right, or bottom) and a scrollable ancestor with enough space.

11. top, right, bottom, left

These properties only work on positioned elements (not static). They set the distance from the respective edge.

.box {
  position: absolute;
  top: 0;
  left: 0;
  /* Top-left corner of the positioning parent */
}

.center {
  position: fixed;
  top: 50%;
  left: 50%;
  transform: translate(-50%, -50%);
  /* Centered on the screen */
}

12. z-index (Stacking)

When positioned elements overlap, z-index decides who is on top. Higher z-index wins.

.behind { position: absolute; z-index: 1; }
.middle { position: absolute; z-index: 10; }
.front  { position: absolute; z-index: 100; }

.modal  { position: fixed; z-index: 9999; }

Important rules:

  • z-index only works on positioned elements (not static)
  • It creates stacking contexts -- children are layered relative to their parent's stack
  • You cannot escape a parent's stacking context. A child with z-index: 9999 inside a parent with z-index: 1 can never go above a sibling of that parent with z-index: 2.

13. overflow

Controls what happens when content is bigger than its container.

.box { overflow: visible; }  /* default: content spills out */
.box { overflow: hidden; }   /* clip content outside the box */
.box { overflow: scroll; }   /* always show scrollbars */
.box { overflow: auto; }     /* scrollbars only when needed (best default) */

.box { overflow-x: auto; overflow-y: hidden; } /* different per axis */

codeExamples.title: display: block, inline, inline-block
codeExamples.code:
Display types

I am a block. I take the full width and start on a new line.
Another block. Also full width, new line.

In this paragraph by Aarav, the words span 1 and span 2 are inline. They flow with the text and ignore width/height.

IB 1 IB 2 IB 3 IB 4 (inline-block: flows in a row, but accepts width/height/margin)
codeExamples.explanation: Three display types compared. Blocks stack vertically taking full width. Inline elements flow with text and ignore width/height. Inline-block elements flow horizontally but accept sizing properties -- perfect for old-school button rows.
codeExamples.output: Two yellow full-width blocks, a paragraph with purple-highlighted inline text, and a row of 4 green inline-block boxes.

codeExamples.title: display: none vs visibility: hidden
codeExamples.code:
Row A - all visible

A
B
C
D

Row B - B has display: none

A
B
C
D

Notice: no gap where B was. It is completely removed from layout.

Row C - B has visibility: hidden

A
B
C
D

Notice: empty space where B would be. Space is reserved.

By Priya Kapoor
codeExamples.explanation: This shows the exact visual difference between the two. In row B (display: none), B disappears and C slides next to A. In row C (visibility: hidden), B is invisible but its box is still reserved, so C stays in its original position.
codeExamples.output: Three rows of purple boxes. Row A has 4 visible. Row B has 3 with no gap. Row C has 3 with an empty gap where the second box would be.

codeExamples.title: position: absolute Inside relative
codeExamples.code:
NEW
SALE

Coding Kit

Everything Kavya needs to learn HTML, CSS, and JavaScript in 12 weeks.
codeExamples.explanation: The card has position: relative so it becomes the positioning context. The badge uses position: absolute with negative offsets to hang off the top-right corner. The ribbon is similarly positioned on the left. This is the exact pattern for product cards with corner badges.
codeExamples.output: A white product card with a red circular 'SALE' badge in the top-right corner (slightly outside the card) and a purple 'NEW' ribbon on the left side.

codeExamples.title: Sticky Navbar
codeExamples.code:
SiteName
Home About Contact

Section 1

Scroll down and watch the nav stay pinned to the top. By Ishaan.

Section 2

The nav uses position: sticky with top: 0.

Section 3

It scrolls normally until it reaches the top, then it sticks there.

Section 4

z-index: 100 ensures the nav stays above other content that scrolls underneath.
codeExamples.explanation: position: sticky makes the nav behave like a normal element until the scroll position reaches top: 0, then it sticks to the top of the viewport. No JavaScript needed. z-index keeps it above other content.
codeExamples.output: A page with a navbar that scrolls with the content initially, then sticks to the top when you scroll past it.

codeExamples.title: Modal Overlay (Fixed Positioning)
codeExamples.code:
Page with Modal

The modal below uses position: fixed to cover the entire viewport, even when you scroll.

Scroll down and notice the modal stays centered on the screen. Fixed positioning does not move with scroll.

The dark background is an overlay. The white card inside is centered using flexbox on the overlay.

By Riya Singh. The close button uses absolute positioning relative to the modal itself.

Welcome, Kavya!

This is a modal dialog. It sits on top of the entire page using fixed positioning.
codeExamples.explanation: The overlay uses position: fixed with all four edges set to 0, covering the whole viewport. Flexbox centers the modal inside it. The close button uses position: absolute in the top-right corner of the modal. Scrolling the page does not move the modal at all.
codeExamples.output: A page content behind a dark overlay with a white modal card centered on screen. Scrolling does not affect the modal position.

codeExamples.title: z-index Stacking
codeExamples.code:
Stacking with z-index

A (z:1)
B (z:2)
C (z:3)
D (z:2)

Higher z-index appears on top. Meera arranged these.
codeExamples.explanation: Four absolutely positioned boxes overlap. Their z-index values determine stacking order: C (3) on top, B and D tie at 2 (D wins because it comes later in source order), A at 1 on the bottom. Higher z-index always appears over lower, but only among positioned elements.
codeExamples.output: Four overlapping colored squares stacked diagonally, with the purple 'C' on top and the red 'A' at the bottom.

commonMistakes.title: Setting width/height on inline Elements
commonMistakes.error: inline elements ignore width and height. The link stays its natural content size.
commonMistakes.explanation: If you need to set explicit dimensions, change the display to inline-block or block. Only block-level elements respect width and height.

commonMistakes.title: z-index Not Working on Static Elements
commonMistakes.error: z-index only works on positioned elements. A static element ignores z-index.
commonMistakes.explanation: z-index requires position to be anything except static. Add position: relative to give z-index effect without moving the element.

commonMistakes.title: Absolute Positioning Without a Positioned Ancestor
commonMistakes.wrongCode:
I'm inside the parent!
/* No position on parent. Child is positioned relative to the viewport, not the parent. */
commonMistakes.error: The child jumps to the viewport's top-left, not the parent's.
commonMistakes.correctCode:
.parent { position: relative; }
.child  { position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; }
commonMistakes.explanation: Absolute elements are positioned relative to the nearest POSITIONED ancestor. If none exists, they use the viewport. Always add position: relative to the parent when you want to anchor an absolute child to it.

commonMistakes.title: position: sticky Without a Threshold or Scrollable Context
commonMistakes.error: Without a threshold, sticky behaves like relative and never actually sticks.
commonMistakes.explanation: Sticky requires a threshold value (top, right, bottom, or left) to know when to stick. Also the ancestor must be taller than the sticky element to actually allow scrolling.

summary: display: block starts on a new line and takes full width (divs, headings, paragraphs). display: inline flows with text and ignores width/height (spans, links). display: inline-block flows horizontally but respects width/height.
display: none removes the element from layout (other elements move up). visibility: hidden makes it invisible but preserves its space (other elements stay put).
position: static is the default (normal flow). position: relative lets you offset with top/right/bottom/left while keeping the element in normal flow.
position: absolute removes the element from flow and places it relative to the nearest positioned ancestor (or the viewport if none exists).
position: fixed pins the element to the VIEWPORT -- it stays in place when you scroll. Perfect for floating buttons, modal overlays, and always-visible navbars.
position: sticky is a hybrid: behaves like relative until you scroll past its threshold (like top: 0), then sticks like fixed. Great for sticky headers and table rows.
top, right, bottom, and left only work on positioned elements (not static). They set offsets from the respective edges.
z-index controls stacking order when positioned elements overlap. Higher z-index appears on top. It does not work on static elements. It creates stacking contexts that contain their children.
A common pattern: parent has position: relative, child has position: absolute with top/left/right/bottom. The child is placed relative to the parent.
overflow controls what happens when content exceeds its box: visible (default, spills out), hidden (clipped), scroll (always show scrollbars), auto (scrollbars when needed).

topicSpecific.question: Which display value starts an element on a new line and takes full width?
topicSpecific.answer: block
topicSpecific.explanation: block elements start on a new line and fill the available width of their parent. divs, headings, paragraphs, sections all default to block.

topicSpecific.question: What happens if you set width and height on display: inline?
topicSpecific.answer: Nothing. Inline elements ignore width and height. They only take the size of their content.
topicSpecific.explanation: inline elements flow like text. Only block and inline-block elements respect explicit width and height.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between display: none and visibility: hidden?
topicSpecific.answer: display: none removes the element from the layout entirely (other elements move up). visibility: hidden keeps the space but makes it invisible.
topicSpecific.explanation: Use display: none when you want the element gone. Use visibility: hidden when you want it invisible but the layout should not shift.

topicSpecific.question: What is the default position value?
topicSpecific.answer: static
topicSpecific.explanation: All elements default to position: static. top/right/bottom/left and z-index have no effect on static elements.

topicSpecific.question: Does position: relative remove the element from normal flow?
topicSpecific.hint: It still leaves a ghost where it was.
topicSpecific.answer: No. The element still takes its original space. Any top/right/bottom/left offset moves only the visual position.
topicSpecific.explanation: relative elements flow normally but can be nudged visually with offsets. Other elements continue to treat the original position as taken.

topicSpecific.question: What does position: fixed anchor the element to?
topicSpecific.answer: The viewport (browser window). It stays in the same screen position when scrolling.
topicSpecific.explanation: Fixed elements are positioned relative to the browser viewport, not any parent element. Perfect for floating action buttons and overlays.

topicSpecific.question: What anchors position: absolute?
topicSpecific.answer: The nearest ancestor with position set to anything other than static. If no such ancestor exists, it uses the viewport.
topicSpecific.explanation: Walking up the DOM tree, absolute elements find the first ancestor with position: relative/absolute/fixed/sticky. That becomes their positioning context.

topicSpecific.question: Does z-index work on position: static elements?
topicSpecific.answer: No. z-index only works on positioned elements (relative, absolute, fixed, sticky).
topicSpecific.explanation: To give z-index effect, you must first set a position value other than static. Usually position: relative is enough if you don't need offsets.

topicSpecific.question: What does overflow: auto do?
topicSpecific.answer: Adds scrollbars only when content exceeds the box. If content fits, no scrollbars appear.
topicSpecific.explanation: auto is the most user-friendly overflow setting. It avoids unnecessary scrollbars but allows scrolling when content is too large.

topicSpecific.question: What display value is used for navigation links that should sit in a row and also have padding?
topicSpecific.hint: Flows like text but accepts sizing.
topicSpecific.answer: inline-block
topicSpecific.explanation: inline-block elements flow in a row like inline, but accept width, height, padding, and margin. Classic for navigation items and badges.

topicSpecific.question:
What is the result?
.parent { position: relative; width: 300px; height: 200px; background: #eee; }
.child { position: absolute; top: 10px; right: 10px; }
topicSpecific.hint: Child is pinned relative to the parent.
topicSpecific.answer: The child is pinned to the top-right corner of the parent, 10px from each edge.
topicSpecific.explanation: The parent is positioned (relative), so it becomes the positioning context for the absolute child. top and right set the distance from those edges of the parent.

topicSpecific.hint: top/left moves the top-left corner to center. transform shifts back by half the modal size.
topicSpecific.answer: A perfectly centered modal on the viewport, regardless of its size.
topicSpecific.explanation: top: 50% + left: 50% places the top-left corner at the center. translate(-50%, -50%) shifts the modal back by half its own dimensions, centering it precisely.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this sticky nav do?
nav { position: sticky; top: 0; z-index: 100; }
topicSpecific.hint: Sticky sticks when the threshold is reached.
topicSpecific.answer: Scrolls with the page until it reaches the top of the viewport, then sticks in place while the rest of the page scrolls beneath it.
topicSpecific.explanation: position: sticky is a hybrid of relative and fixed. top: 0 is the threshold. z-index ensures the nav sits above other content as it scrolls underneath.

topicSpecific.answer: .b (z-index 10)
topicSpecific.explanation: Among sibling positioned elements with the same parent, higher z-index wins. b (10) > c (5) > a (1).

topicSpecific.question: What happens to the page when you add position: absolute to an element that was previously in normal flow?
topicSpecific.answer: The element is removed from the layout. Surrounding elements shift up to fill the space it used to take.
topicSpecific.explanation: Absolute elements are taken out of the document flow. The other elements lay out as if the absolute element never existed.

topicSpecific.question: What does overflow: hidden do to content that is bigger than its container?
topicSpecific.answer: Clips (crops) any content that extends beyond the box. No scrollbars appear.
topicSpecific.explanation: overflow: hidden hard-crops overflowing content. Useful for clipping images, creating masked effects, or preventing layout-breaking overflow.

topicSpecific.question:
What does this do to a badge on a card?
.card { position: relative; }
.badge { position: absolute; top: -10px; right: -10px; }
topicSpecific.answer: The badge floats outside the top-right corner of the card, hanging off the edge by 10px in each direction.
topicSpecific.explanation: Negative offsets let absolutely positioned elements extend beyond their parent's boundary. This is a common pattern for corner badges and notification dots.

topicSpecific.question:
Why doesn't this z-index work?
.item { z-index: 999; }
topicSpecific.answer: Because there is no position property set. z-index requires position to be anything except static.
topicSpecific.explanation: Add position: relative (or absolute/fixed/sticky) to enable z-index on the element.

topicSpecific.question: What is the difference between overflow: scroll and overflow: auto?
topicSpecific.answer: scroll ALWAYS shows scrollbars, even if content fits. auto shows scrollbars only when needed.
topicSpecific.explanation: auto is almost always the better choice because it avoids cluttering the UI with unnecessary scrollbars.

topicSpecific.question: How do you position an element in the top-right corner of its parent?
topicSpecific.answer: Set the parent to position: relative and the child to position: absolute; top: 0; right: 0;. The child will be pinned to the parent's top-right.
topicSpecific.explanation: This is the most common positioning pattern: a relative parent acts as the positioning context for an absolute child.

topicSpecific.answer: The child (even with z-index 9999) cannot appear above parent-2. Its z-index is scoped to parent-1's stacking context, and parent-2 (z-index 2) is stacked above parent-1 (z-index 1).
topicSpecific.explanation: Once a parent creates a stacking context, its children cannot escape. The highest z-index inside a parent is still trapped by the parent's own stack level.

topicSpecific.question:
Why does position: sticky; top: 0; not stick?
.parent { overflow: hidden; }
.sticky-child { position: sticky; top: 0; }
topicSpecific.hint: Sticky has a scrollable ancestor requirement.
topicSpecific.answer: Because an ancestor has overflow: hidden, which prevents scrolling and breaks sticky positioning. Sticky needs a scrollable ancestor.
topicSpecific.explanation: Sticky elements need a scrollable context. overflow: hidden, auto, or scroll on an ancestor can either help or break sticky depending on whether the ancestor provides scrolling. overflow: hidden with no scrolling often breaks sticky.

topicSpecific.question: Which value moves ONLY the visual position but keeps the element in normal flow?
topicSpecific.answer: position: relative with top/right/bottom/left offsets. The element's original space is preserved; only the rendered position shifts.
topicSpecific.explanation: relative is unique: it lets you offset visually without removing the element from flow. Great for fine-tuning placement without disrupting layout.

topicSpecific.question: When should you use position: fixed vs position: sticky?
topicSpecific.hint: Always visible vs only visible after scrolling.
topicSpecific.answer: Use fixed when an element must always be visible at the same screen position (floating action buttons, modal overlays). Use sticky when an element should scroll normally until it reaches a threshold, then stay pinned (sticky navbars, section headers, table headers).
topicSpecific.explanation: Fixed is always detached from flow. Sticky is context-aware: it scrolls with its parent until the threshold, then sticks. Sticky feels more integrated with the page.

topicSpecific.question: What is a stacking context and how is it created?
topicSpecific.answer: A stacking context is a self-contained layer where child elements are stacked relative to each other. It is created by: the root element, any positioned element with z-index other than auto, elements with opacity less than 1, elements with transform, filter, or certain other properties. Children can never escape their parent's stacking context.
topicSpecific.explanation: Understanding stacking contexts explains why high z-index values sometimes fail to appear on top. The element is inside a parent stacking context that is lower than the competing sibling.

mixed.question: Does display: block accept width and height?
mixed.answer: Yes. Block elements respect both width and height (unlike inline).
mixed.explanation: Block elements are full-featured: width, height, padding, margin, and border all work as expected.

mixed.question: Which display makes an element disappear AND free up its space?
mixed.answer: display: none
mixed.explanation: display: none removes the element from the layout entirely. Its space collapses and other elements move in.

mixed.question: Which position keeps an element on screen while scrolling?
mixed.answer: fixed (always) and sticky (after reaching a threshold).
mixed.explanation: Fixed is pinned to the viewport always. Sticky scrolls normally until hitting its threshold, then pins.

mixed.question: What anchors a position: relative element?
mixed.answer: Its own original position in normal flow. top/right/bottom/left offset it from where it would have been.
mixed.explanation: relative does not change the layout. It just lets you shift the visual rendering. The element's slot in the flow is unchanged.

mixed.question: How do you hide something but keep its space?
mixed.answer: visibility: hidden
mixed.explanation: The element becomes invisible but still occupies its original space. Surrounding elements do not shift.

mixed.question: Which CSS property controls how content bigger than its box is handled?
mixed.answer: overflow
mixed.explanation: overflow values: visible (default, spills out), hidden (clipped), scroll (always shows scrollbars), auto (scrollbars when needed).

mixed.question:
What does this do?
img { display: block; margin: 0 auto; }
mixed.answer: Displays the image as a block (no inline whitespace issues) and centers it horizontally in its container.
mixed.explanation: Images are inline by default. Making them block allows margin: 0 auto centering. Common pattern for centered figure images.

mixed.question: Can you use top/bottom on a position: static element?
mixed.answer: No. top/right/bottom/left only work on positioned elements (relative, absolute, fixed, sticky).
mixed.explanation: Static elements ignore these properties. You must set position to enable offsets.

mixed.question: What is the behavior of a position: sticky element without a threshold?
mixed.answer: It behaves identically to position: relative. It never actually sticks.
mixed.explanation: Sticky requires at least one of top/right/bottom/left to set a threshold. Without one, it degrades to relative behavior.

mixed.question: How do you make a floating action button in the bottom-right of the page?
mixed.answer: position: fixed; bottom: 30px; right: 30px;
mixed.explanation: Fixed anchors it to the viewport. The bottom and right offsets place it 30px from the corresponding edges. It stays put during scrolling.

mixed.question: What is inline-block best for?
mixed.answer: Elements that should flow in a row like inline elements but need explicit width, height, or padding. Examples: navigation items, badges, pill buttons, tags.
mixed.explanation: Before flexbox, inline-block was the main way to lay out items in a row. It still has legitimate uses for small components.

mixed.question: Why use position: relative on a parent without offsets?
mixed.answer: To create a positioning context for absolutely positioned children. The parent itself does not move, but any absolute child inside it is anchored to the parent instead of the viewport.
mixed.explanation: This is the most common use of position: relative. It's a container trick: the parent becomes a reference frame.

mixed.question: When should you use overflow: hidden vs overflow: auto?
mixed.answer: Use hidden to clip overflowing content completely (no scrollbars, content is cut off). Use auto to allow users to scroll through overflowing content with scrollbars.
mixed.explanation: hidden is for containment and design. auto is for scrollable content areas like feeds or chat windows.

mixed.question: How would you build a corner badge on a product card?
mixed.hint: Parent positioned relative, badge absolute with negative offsets.
mixed.answer: Set the card to position: relative. Add a badge element with position: absolute; top: -8px; right: -8px;. The badge will hang off the card's top-right corner.
mixed.explanation: Negative offsets allow absolutely positioned elements to extend beyond their parent's bounds, perfect for corner decorations.

mixed.question: Why is z-index 9999 sometimes not enough to put an element on top?
mixed.answer: Because z-index is scoped to the parent's stacking context. If the parent has a lower z-index than another parent's stack, no child inside can escape. The child is trapped below the other stack.
mixed.explanation: Check if any ancestor has opacity, transform, filter, or position + z-index. These create stacking contexts that trap children.

mixed.question:
What is the final positioned area?
.wrap { position: relative; width: 400px; height: 300px; }
.layer { position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; right: 0; bottom: 0; }
mixed.answer: The layer fills the entire .wrap element (400x300), covering it completely.
mixed.explanation: Setting top, right, bottom, and left all to 0 on an absolute element makes it stretch to fill the positioning parent. Classic overlay technique.

mixed.question:
What does this produce?
.tooltip { position: absolute; bottom: 100%; left: 50%; transform: translateX(-50%); }
mixed.answer: Positions the tooltip directly above its positioned parent, horizontally centered on the parent.
mixed.explanation: bottom: 100% places the element's bottom at the parent's top. left: 50% plus translateX(-50%) centers it horizontally. A tooltip-above-button pattern.

mixed.question: Why use transform: translate(-50%, -50%) for centering?
mixed.hint: Offsets by 50% of the element's own size.
mixed.answer: When combined with top: 50% and left: 50%, this shifts the element back by half its own width and height, centering it exactly. Percentage translations refer to the element's own size, so -50% always works regardless of the element's dimensions.
mixed.explanation: A classic centering pattern: top/left 50% puts the corner at center, transform: translate(-50%, -50%) adjusts to put the center at center.

mixed.question: What are the 4 common positioning patterns?
mixed.answer: (1) Sticky navbar (position: sticky; top: 0). (2) Modal overlay (position: fixed covering viewport + centered child). (3) Corner badge (relative parent + absolute child with negative offsets). (4) Floating action button (position: fixed; bottom+right).
mixed.explanation: Almost every layout challenge reduces to one of these patterns. Memorize them and you can handle most UI positioning tasks.

mixed.question: Why does display: none not animate with CSS transitions?
mixed.answer: display is a non-animatable property -- it changes instantly between values with no intermediate states. To fade elements in and out, use opacity and visibility (both animatable) instead.
mixed.explanation: For fade effects, set opacity: 0 and visibility: hidden together, and transition both. The element hides and shows smoothly instead of popping.

mcqs.question: Which display value is default for a div?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. divs are block by default. They start on a new line and take full width.

mcqs.question: Which display value is default for a span?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. spans are inline by default. They flow with text and ignore width/height.

mcqs.question: Which completely removes an element from layout?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. display: none removes the element from the document flow. Other elements act as if it does not exist.

mcqs.question: Which keeps the element invisible but preserves its layout space?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. visibility: hidden and opacity: 0 both preserve space, but visibility: hidden is the official 'invisible but still there' answer.

mcqs.question: Which is the default position value?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. All elements default to static. top/right/bottom/left have no effect on static elements.

mcqs.question: Which position value pins an element to the viewport?
mcqs.explanation: D is correct. fixed elements stay in the same screen position regardless of scrolling.

mcqs.question: Which position value is a hybrid of relative and fixed?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. sticky behaves like relative until you scroll past its threshold, then like fixed.

mcqs.question: Does z-index work on static elements?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. z-index requires position to be something other than static.

mcqs.question: Which overflow value shows scrollbars only when needed?
mcqs.explanation: D is correct. auto only shows scrollbars when content overflows. scroll always shows them even if content fits.

mcqs.question: Which display value flows horizontally AND accepts width/height?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. inline-block is the best of both worlds: horizontal flow with full box model support.

mcqs.question: An absolutely positioned element is positioned relative to...

options.B: Its nearest ancestor that has position set to anything other than static

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Absolute elements use the nearest positioned ancestor. If there is none, they fall back to the viewport.

mcqs.question:
What happens without a position on the parent?
.child { position: absolute; top: 0; left: 0; }

options.A: The child is positioned at the viewport's top-left

mcqs.explanation: A is correct. Without a positioned ancestor, absolute elements are positioned relative to the viewport.

mcqs.question: What does overflow: hidden do?

options.B: Crops content that extends beyond the box

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. overflow: hidden clips overflowing content. No scrollbars appear.

mcqs.question: How do you perfectly center a fixed modal using top/left/transform?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. top/left 50% places the top-left corner at center. transform: translate(-50%, -50%) shifts the modal back by half its own size to center it exactly.

mcqs.question: Which position value does NOT remove the element from flow?
mcqs.explanation: A is correct. relative keeps the element in normal flow. absolute and fixed remove it.

mcqs.question: What value does position: sticky need to work?

options.B: At least one of top/right/bottom/left as a threshold

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Sticky requires a threshold value to know when to stick. Without one it behaves like relative.

mcqs.question: What does display: inline ignore?
mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Inline elements ignore width and height. They size to their content only.

mcqs.question: Which creates a new stacking context?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. A positioned element with a non-auto z-index creates a new stacking context. Transform, opacity less than 1, and filters also create them.

mcqs.question: Which CSS makes a navbar stick to the top when scrolling?
mcqs.explanation: D is correct. Both work. fixed always pins to viewport. sticky scrolls normally first, then sticks. sticky is often preferred because it feels more integrated.

mcqs.question: How do you position a badge in the top-right corner of a card?

options.C: position: absolute; top: 0; right: 0 (with positioned parent)

mcqs.explanation: C is correct. Set the card to position: relative. Add the badge with position: absolute; top: 0; right: 0.

mcqs.question: Why might z-index: 9999 still not put an element on top?

options.B: It is trapped in a parent stacking context that is below the competing element's parent

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Child z-index is scoped to the parent stacking context. A high child z-index cannot escape a parent that is stacked lower.

mcqs.question: How do you make an overlay cover the entire viewport?

options.B: position: fixed; top: 0; left: 0; right: 0; bottom: 0;

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Fixed positioning with all four edges at 0 fills the viewport. Classic modal overlay pattern.

mcqs.question: Which pair prevents layout shift when hiding/showing on hover?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. visibility preserves the element's space. display: none removes it entirely, causing layout shifts.

mcqs.question: Why does sticky fail when an ancestor has overflow: hidden?

options.B: Sticky needs a scrollable ancestor; overflow: hidden prevents scrolling in that ancestor and breaks the stick

mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Sticky requires a scrollable parent context. overflow: hidden without scrolling ability can break sticky.

mcqs.question: Which pattern floats a button in the bottom-right of the viewport?
mcqs.explanation: B is correct. Fixed positioning with bottom and right offsets places the button in the bottom-right of the viewport, persistent during scrolling.

codingChallenges.title: Inline vs Block vs Inline-Block Demo
codingChallenges.description: Create a page with three sections, each showing a row of 4 colored boxes using different display values: block, inline, and inline-block. Label each row clearly.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: Three labeled rows demonstrating how each display value affects layout.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use div and span with class names. Make the visual difference clear.
codingChallenges.solution:
display: block (stacks vertically)

Block 1
Block 2

display: inline (ignores width/height)

By Aarav: Inline 1 Inline 2 flow with text.

display: inline-block (row + sizing)

IB 1 IB 2 IB 3 IB 4

codingChallenges.title: Sticky Navbar
codingChallenges.description: Build a page with a sticky navbar that stays at the top when scrolling. The navbar should have a logo on the left and 3 links on the right. Below, add enough content to enable scrolling.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: The nav scrolls with the page initially, then sticks to the top.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use position: sticky; top: 0. Add z-index so the nav stays above the content.
codingChallenges.solution:
Kavya.dev
HomeBlogContact

Section 1

Scroll down. The nav stays at the top because position: sticky.

Section 2

It keeps sticking as you scroll further through the page.

Section 3

z-index keeps it above the section content.

codingChallenges.title: Floating Action Button
codingChallenges.description: Build a page with a long content area and a circular floating action button (FAB) fixed in the bottom-right of the viewport. The button should always be visible while scrolling.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A purple circle button stays pinned to the bottom-right of the screen.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use position: fixed. Make the button circular with border-radius: 50%.
codingChallenges.solution:
Long Article

The purple + button at the bottom-right stays fixed as you scroll. Try it, Ishaan!

Fixed positioning anchors the button to the viewport instead of the document.

It does not move when you scroll, unlike relative or absolute positioning.

Common use: floating action buttons, chat widgets, cookie banners.

Scroll down to see more content and observe the FAB staying in place.

Fixed elements ignore their parents and anchor directly to the viewport edges.

Keep scrolling to see the effect clearly.

More content here for scrolling demonstration.

codingChallenges.title: Modal Overlay
codingChallenges.description: Build a modal dialog that sits on top of the entire page. Use a dark semi-transparent overlay covering the viewport and a white modal card centered on screen. Include a close button in the modal's top-right corner.
codingChallenges.sampleOutput: A modal with a dark overlay and centered white card with a close button.
codingChallenges.constraints: Overlay uses position: fixed with all edges at 0. Modal is centered with flex. Close button uses position: absolute.
codingChallenges.solution:
Home Page by Meera

Behind the overlay, regular content. The modal is always centered on screen.

Welcome!

This modal uses fixed positioning for the overlay and absolute positioning for the close button.

codingChallenges.sampleOutput: An image card with title text overlayed at the bottom inside a semi-transparent gradient.
codingChallenges.constraints: Use position: relative on the image wrapper and position: absolute on the text overlay.
